---
title: "Day 2: Command Line Interface (CLI) and Your AI Tutor"
subtitle: "Morning brief, Tuesday July 14"
author: "Dan Negrut"
date: 2026-07-14
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    width: 1250
    margin: 0.08
    transition: fade
    footer: "PROCSI C Training  |  Day 2"
    embed-resources: true
---

## Where this fits

- Almost everything this camp does runs through the **terminal**: git, Python, conda, PyChrono.
- Today you get comfortable driving the computer by typing, and you meet the helper you'll lean on all camp: **Claude**.
- No prior experience needed. Poke at things and see what happens.

## Today's target

- **By 3:30, your team can:** open a terminal, move around the filesystem, create / rename / delete files and folders, and `git clone` a repository, all from the command line. 
- **Bonus:** you've had a real back-and-forth with Claude and used it to figure something out on your own.
- Herein, I say "Claude", but "ChatGPT" is fine, too.

## How to start

- Open a terminal: **Terminal** on macOS, **Windows Terminal** or **PowerShell** on Windows.
- **No starter today.** Start from a blank prompt; exploring is the point.
- Meet Claude, and try asks like:
  - "I'm on [macOS / Windows]. Explain `pwd`, `ls`, `cd`, `mkdir` with tiny examples."
  - Paste any error you hit and ask "what does this mean and how do I fix it?"

## Command map {.smaller}

- `pwd` where am I &nbsp;•&nbsp; `ls` what's here &nbsp;•&nbsp; `cd <dir>` move in &nbsp;•&nbsp; `cd ..` up one
- `mkdir <name>` make a folder &nbsp;•&nbsp; `touch <file>` make a file
- `mv <a> <b>` rename or move &nbsp;•&nbsp; `rm <file>` delete (careful, no undo)
- `git clone <url>` copy a repository onto your machine
- Handy: **Tab** completes names, **Up arrow** recalls your last command

## Your end-of-day talk

- ~10 minutes: what you explored, the handiest command you found, and one thing Claude helped you understand.
- **Start or Close with your team's quote of the day.**
- Stretch goal: find and explain one command not on the map above (for example `cat`, `grep`, or `history`).
